coredata.dc:title: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing

Wang, Ruixue
Li, Hao
Shao, Jingwei
Chi, Yuwu
Lin, Xiaomei
Chen, Guonan
coredata.dc:description: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (42.5%) have been synthesized by the low temperature (<200°C) carbonization of citric acid with branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) in one simple step. The obtained BPEI–CQDs are spherical graphite nanocrystals (average 6.2nm in size) capped with abundant BPEI at their surfaces. It is the first report that CQDs are both amino-functionalized and highly fluorescent, which suggests their promising applications in chemical sensing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of the BPEI-functionalized CQDs.
XPS survey (a) and C 1s spectra (b) obtained for the BPEI–CQD.
(a) XRD pattern of the BPEI–CQDs. (b) FT-IR spectra of the CA, BPEI and PEI–CQDs.
HRTEM image (a) and size distribution (b) of the BPEI–CQDs.

Optical properties of BPEI–CQDs. (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CA, BPEI and BPEI–CQDs, and FL spectra of the BPEI–CQDs. Inset: Photographs of the as-prepared BPEI–CQD solution under a visible light (left) and a UV beam of 365nm (right). (b) Effect of solution pH on the FL intensity of the as-prepared BPEI–CQDs.

head.ce:title: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou Univiersity, Fujian 350108, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (42.5%) have been synthesized by the low temperature (<200°C) carbonization of citric acid with branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) in one simple step. The obtained BPEI–CQDs are spherical graphite nanocrystals (average 6.2nm in size) capped with abundant BPEI at their surfaces. It is the first report that CQDs are both amino-functionalized and highly fluorescent, which suggests their promising applications in chemical sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), semiconductor nanocrystals with dimensions smaller than the bulk exciton Bohr radius [1], exhibit unique quantum size effects and strongly size dependent electronic, optical, electrochemical (EC) properties [1–4]. One of the most important properties of QDs is their fluorescence (FL). Compared with organic fluorescent agents, QDs usually have much higher photo stability and are resistant to photobleaching. Accordingly, QDs has been well applied in bioimaging [3,5] and biosensing [6–8] based on their FL property. However, most of the high-performance QDs require the use of heavy metal elements such as cadmium, which may represent serious limitations for their applications due to the known toxicity and potential environmental hazard of cadmium. Therefore, the search for more benign substitutes of semiconductor QDs is worthwhile and urgent.

Recently emerging carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs), usually referring to carbon nanocrystals of less than 10nm in size, not only exhibit many fascinating optical properties, such as photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer, and electrochemiluminescence (ECL), but also present some additional advantages over the heavy metal-containing semiconductor-based QDs, such as chemical inertness, biocompatibility and low toxicity [9]. Therefore, CQDs may become very promising substitutes of semiconductor-based QDs, and have attracted more and more attention in the past seven years [9–23]. Up to date, most researches have been focused on the synthetic methods of CQDs. The developed methods can be classified into two types, i.e. “top-down” methods [10–19] and “bottom-up” approaches [20–23]. The top-down methods prepare CQDs from larger carbon materials, such as nanodiamonds [10], graphite [11–14], carbon nanotubes [15,16], carbon soots [17], activate carbon [18], graphite oxide [19], while the bottom-up approaches synthesize CQDs from molecular precursors such as citric acid [20], glucose [21,22], and resin [23]. Recently, with rapid advances in CQD research, the functionalization of CQDs and their applications, especially in bioimaging have become a hot topic after the researches on CQD synthesis. Sun and co-workers have used PPEI–EI and PGE1500N-functionalized CQDs for bioimaging [11–13]. However, very few functionalized CQDs have been prepared for chemical sensing, which limits the application of CQDs in analytical chemistry. This is probably due to the difficulty in finding functionalized CQDs that can both selectively recognize a target and give sensitive signal response.

Therefore, we try to synthesize amino-functionalized CQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) by using low temperature bottom-up synthesis method. The design in CQD synthesis is based on the following considerations: well surface passivation of CQDs plays a key role in quantum dots, however, in most bottom-up approaches, reaction temperatures are quite high (usually >300°C) to ensure complete carbonization of molecular precursors, which may destroy the organic capping reagents and thus lead to bad surface passivation, and loss (or denaturation) of functional groups. Therefore the use of low temperature for both carbonization and passivation would beneficial to obtain both well passivated and functionalized CQDs. Herein, we choose citric acid (CA) as the carbon source due to its well-known low carbonization temperature (<200°C), and use a branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) as the capping and functionalizing reagent since the polyamine structure allows BPEI to passivate CQDs with some amines and recognize analytes with the other free amines. The principle for the synthesis of the BPEI-capped CQDs, or the BPEI-functionalized CQDs (BPEI–CQDs) is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

CA was purchased from Alfa Aesar. BPEI with molecular weight of 1800Da was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were analytically pure and used as received. The phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH values were prepared by titrating 0.1molL−1 phosphoric acid solution with a concentrated sodium hydroxide solution (5molL−1) to required pH values.

The BPEI–CQDs were synthesized by an easy one-step and low temperature pyrolysis of CA in the presence of BPEI. In a typical synthesis procedure, 0.5g BPEI and 1.0g CA were dissolved uniformly with 10mL hot water in a 25mL beaker, and then heated moderately (<200°C) using a heating mantle. It should be mentioned that the temperature should not be higher than 200°C, avoiding the degeneration of BPEI. About 20min later, most water was evaporated, remaining a uniform pale-yellow gel. Added 1mL water before the gel was scorched and kept heating. The same procedure was repeated for 10 times (in 3h), the color of gel turned to orange, suggesting the formation of BPEI–CQDs. Finally, the obtained BPEI–CQDs were adjusted to 10mL solution using doubly distilled water. The resultant BPEI–CQD solution was purified by silica gel column chromatography with 0.01molL−1 HCl solution as the developing solvent. The purified BPEI–CQD solution was stored under 4°C before further study. The pH 7 PBS for the ECL experiments of BPEI–CQDs was added with 0.5molL−1 Na2SO4 to improve the conductibility of the testing solution.

The quantum yield of the BPEI–CQDs was measured by following equation:
(1)
Q =
Q
R
I
I
R
OD
R
OD
n
2
n
R
2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and OD is the optical density, which is measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield, for an example, quinine sulfate (QS) used in present work. The quinine sulfate (literature Φ =0.54) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (n =1.33) and the BPEI–CQDs was dissolved in distilled water (n =1.33).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data for C 1s of the BPEI–CQD solution deposited on a glass substrate was measured by a ESCAlab 250 XPS system having an Al K source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Japan Rigaku D/max-3C using Cu Ka radiation. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were performed on an electronic microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200KV). UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). All FL spectra were obtained by a FL spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse Varian). EC and ECL signals were measured simultaneously by an ECL & EC multi-functional detection system (MPI-E, Remex Electronic Instrument Lt. Co., Xi’an, China) equipped with a three-electrode system (a 0.3cm2 Pt wire working electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode).

The XPS result shows that the as-prepared BPEI–CQDs contain mainly carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, the characteristic peak of C–C sp2 appearing at 284.5eV suggests that abundant graphite structures should be contained in the obtained BPEI–CQDs (Fig. 2b). The XRD pattern measured for the BPEI–CQDs shows a broad peak centered at 0.41nm (Fig. 3 a), which is consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with turbostratic disorder [24]. The FT-IR spectrum was also used to characterize the BPEI–CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, the BPEI–CQDs have many characteristic absorption bands of BPEI (NH of 3440 and 1585cm−1, CH2 of 2950 and 2820cm−1, CN of 1122cm−1), but nearly no characteristic absorption of CA. These results indicate that CA might be mostly carbonized during the pyrolysis, while the BPEI kept stable. Furthermore, a new sharp peak associated with amide linkage (–CONH–) found at 1700cm−1 indicates that abundant BPEI should be coated at the surface of the CQDs by the amide linkages. HRTEM imaging (Fig. 4 a) reveals that the as-prepared BPEI–CQDs are monodisperse nanocrystals of near spherical morphology with an average diameter of 6.2nm (Fig. 4b). The BPEI–CQDs nanocrystals have lattice spacing of 0.212nm (see the inset of Fig. 4a), which is consistent with that of (100) facet of graphite. Apparently, the above characterization data indicate that the as-prepared CQDs are BPEI-functionalized graphite nanocrystals.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the BPEI–CQD solution has two evident absorption bands at 360 and 245nm, respectively, while CA and BPEI have nearly no absorption above 240nm. The BPEI–CQD solution shows bright blue luminescence under excitation of 365nm. The FLQY (excited at 365nm) of the BPEI-functionalized CQDs in a pH 4.0 phosphate solution was calculated to be ca. 42.5%, which is much higher than the FLQY values of other CQDs obtained by bottom-up methods [20–23]. The high FLQY of the prepared BPEI–CQDs means that the surfaces of the CQDs are well passivated, since it has been reported that CQDs without passivation have very weak FL emission but they can exhibit strong FL activities after appropriate passivation treatments [11], i.e. the better surface passivation, the higher FL activity of CQDs can be obtained. Probably, the well surface passivation found for the BPEI–CQDs can be explained as follows: the low temperature (<200°C) pyrolysis allows complete carbonization of CA without the destruction of BPEI in the capping layer, moreover, as indicated by FT-IR, there is strong linkage (–CONH–) between the –COOH groups of carbonized CA and –NH2 groups of BPEI, providing a well coating way. The maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength were detected to be 360 and 460nm respectively. Interestingly, the emission spectrum of the solution is nearly excitation-independent. It has been proposed that both surface state and size of CQDs may affect the emission spectra of polymer-passivated CQDs [11]. However, for the presently prepared BPEI–CQDs, the surface states of CQDs may be the same and play the key role in affecting the FL wavelength, since the emission spectrum of the BPEI–CQDs is almost excitation-independent while the size of obtained BPEI–CQDs is not really uniform (it distributes in the range from 4 to 10nm). Similar finding has also been reported by Hu and co-workers [25]. The key role of surface state in the FL of CQDs is further supported by the results obtained by Zheng and co-workers [26], i.e. when the CQDs were reduced, the size of the CQDs had no obvious change, but the emission wavelength shifted from 520 to 450nm, indicating the key role of surface state in the FL emission of CQDs. The FL activity of the BPEI–CQDs was found to be pH-dependent (Fig. 5b). The BPEI–CQDs show good FL activities over a wide pH range (pH 2.0–8.0), with a maximum activity at pH 4.0. However, the FL intensity decreases obviously at pH higher than 9.0. The pH-dependent FL property of the BPEI–CQDs may be attributed to the protonization of the amino groups at their surfaces. The BPEI is positively charged below pH 9.0 since the pKa associated with the amino groups in the BPEI (with molecular weight of 1800 Dalton) is ca. 9.0 [27]. The lower pH, the more positive charges are carried by the BPEI. Therefore the stability of the BPEI–CQDs and thus FL activities increases with decreasing pH due to their electrostatic repulsion. However, it is unclear why the FL intensity of BPEI–CQDs decreases again in the strongly acidic media (pH<4). Probably, too much positive charge carried by BPEI at the CQD surfaces might inhibit the excited states of the BPEI–CQDs. Apparently, the BPEI–CQDs show good FL activities over the pH range 2.0–8.0, suggesting that the polyamine-functionalized CQDs can be used in a relative wide pH range.

As well known, ECL is an important technique to probe the surface states of QDs [28]. Consequently, ECL responses of the obtained BPEI–CQDs were also investigated to further understand their surfaces. The experimental results show that the BPEI–CQDs have very poor ECL activity, i.e. only very weak ECL signals (data is not shown) can be detected even in the presence of K2S2O8 (a commonly used co-reactant agent in ECL reactions). For QDs including CQDs, the ECL activities are mainly determined by their surface states, and thus the surface passivation that directly changes surface states can significantly affect the ECL activities of QDs [14,18,28]. It has been reported that the ECL activities are sensitive to surface passivation, i.e. the CQDs without surface passivation have excellent ECL activities, while those passivated with capping reagents exhibit weak ECL activities, in other words, the better surface passivation, the weaker ECL activities are found for CQDs [18]. Evidently, the surface passivation in CQDs has opposite effects on the ECL and FL activities. In the present study, almost no ECL activity can be found for BPEI–CQDs, which indicates in another aspect that the BPEI-functionalized CQDs should be well passivated at their surfaces.

Finally, the interactions between the as-prepared polyamine-functionalized CQDs and metal ions were investigated for evaluating possible applications of the BPEI–CQDs in chemical sensing. Preliminary investigation shows that the bright BPEI–CQDs FL can be significantly inhibited by some heavy metals, especially Cu2+ ion, suggesting a promising application of the BPEI–CQDs in metal ion chemical sensing. Further detailed study on the chemical sensing of Cu2+ by the polyamine-functionalized CQDs will be published elsewhere.

Highly fluorescent and polyamine-functionalized CQDs have been prepared by one-step carbonization of CA followed by coating with BPEI at a low temperature (<200°C). The BPEI–CQDs are graphite nanospheres (6.2nm average diameter) with abundant of amino groups on their surfaces, and show strong FL activity with a high FLQY (42.5%). The polyamine-functionalized CQDs can both selectively recognize certain metal ions, such as Cu2+ ion, and give a very sensitive signal response, showing their promising use in the chemical sensing of metal ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21075018), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Chinese University (NCET-10-0019), and the Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar of Fujian Province, China (2009J06003).
